## Goal

## Deliverables
- CLI rendering logic that attaches templates to event records
- Consistent formatting (TTY + non‑TTY JSON)
- Optional flag `--no-summary` for terse output

## Testing
- Snapshot tests for `wa events` showing:
  - summary lines
  - rendered suggestions
- Error snapshot tests: human‑readable message included

## Acceptance Criteria
- Human CLI shows natural language for every event/error.
- Non‑TTY output includes description fields in JSON.
- No regressions to robot output schema.

 {"id":"wa-0go.4","title":"Event template system: define templates for all event types with interpolation","description":"# Event template system

## Purpose
Define human-readable templates for all event types that can be interpolated with context-specific values.

## Template Structure
```rust
pub struct EventTemplate {
    /// Event type this template matches
    pub event_type: String,
    
    /// Short summary (for lists, notifications)
    pub summary: String,
    
    /// Full description with context
    pub description: String,
    
    /// Variables available for interpolation
    pub context_keys: Vec<ContextKey>,
    
    /// Actionable suggestions
    pub suggestions: Vec<Suggestion>,
    
    /// Severity level
    pub severity: Severity,
}

pub struct ContextKey {
    pub key: String,
    pub description: String,
    pub example: String,
}

pub struct Suggestion {
    pub text: String,
    pub command: Option<String>,      // wa command to run
    pub doc_link: Option<String>,     // Link to docs
}
```

## Template Interpolation
```rust
pub fn interpolate(template: &str, context: &HashMap<String, Value>) -> String {
    let mut result = template.to_string();
    
    // Simple variable replacement: {variable_name}
    for (key, value) in context {
        let placeholder = format\!("{{{}}}", key);
        let replacement = format_value(value);
        result = result.replace(&placeholder, &replacement);
    }
    
    // Conditional blocks: {?variable}...{/?variable}
    result = process_conditionals(&result, context);
    
    // Pluralization: {count|singular|plural}
    result = process_plurals(&result, context);
    
    result
}

fn format_value(value: &Value) -> String {
    match value {
        Value::String(s) => s.clone(),
        Value::Number(n) => format_number(n),
        Value::DateTime(dt) => format_relative_time(dt),
        Value::Duration(d) => format_duration(d),
        Value::Percentage(p) => format\!("{}%", p),
        _ => value.to_string(),
    }
}
```

## Built-in Templates

### Usage Limit Events
```rust
EventTemplate {
    event_type: "codex.usage_limit_warning",
    summary: "Codex at {percent}% of daily limit",
    description: "Your Codex session in pane \"{pane_title}\" has consumed \
                  {percent}% of your daily token limit ({tokens_used} of \
                  {tokens_total} tokens). {?time_remaining}Limit resets in \
                  {time_remaining}.{/?time_remaining}",
    context_keys: vec\![
        ContextKey::new("percent", "Usage percentage", "85"),
        ContextKey::new("tokens_used", "Tokens consumed", "850,000"),
        ContextKey::new("tokens_total", "Total daily limit", "1,000,000"),
        ContextKey::new("pane_title", "Pane title", "Codex - Project"),
        ContextKey::new("time_remaining", "Time until reset", "3 hours"),
    ],
    suggestions: vec\![
        Suggestion::with_command(
            "Switch to a different account",
            "wa accounts switch"
        ),
        Suggestion::with_command(
            "Check usage breakdown",
            "wa analytics breakdown --by account"
        ),
    ],
    severity: Severity::Warning,
}
```

### Rate Limit Events
```rust
EventTemplate {
    event_type: "codex.rate_limit",
    summary: "Rate limited - waiting {wait_time}",
    description: "Codex in pane \"{pane_title}\" hit a rate limit. The API is \
                  throttling requests. {?wait_time}Estimated wait: {wait_time}.{/?wait_time}",
    suggestions: vec\![
        Suggestion::text("This is normal during heavy usage - the agent will resume automatically"),
        Suggestion::with_command("Reduce polling frequency", "wa config set poll_interval 200ms"),
    ],
    severity: Severity::Info,
}
```

### Compaction Events
```rust
EventTemplate {
    event_type: "codex.compaction_request",
    summary: "Compaction needed - context window full",
    description: "The AI agent in pane \"{pane_title}\" has reached its context \
                  limit and is requesting a summary. This is a checkpoint where \
                  the conversation history needs to be compressed.",
    suggestions: vec\![
        Suggestion::text("The workflow will handle this automatically if configured"),
        Suggestion::with_doc("Learn about context management", "/docs/compaction"),
    ],
    severity: Severity::Info,
}
```

## Template Registry
```rust
pub struct TemplateRegistry {
    templates: HashMap<String, EventTemplate>,
    fallback: EventTemplate,
}

impl TemplateRegistry {
    pub fn get(&self, event_type: &str) -> &EventTemplate {
        self.templates.get(event_type)
            .unwrap_or(&self.fallback)
    }
    
    pub fn render(&self, event: &Detection) -> RenderedEvent {
        let template = self.get(&event.event_type);
        let context = event.to_context();
        
        RenderedEvent {
            summary: interpolate(&template.summary, &context),
            description: interpolate(&template.description, &context),
            suggestions: template.suggestions.iter()
                .map(|s| render_suggestion(s, &context))
                .collect(),
            severity: template.severity,
        }
    }
}
```

- Unit tests for interpolation logic
- Tests for each template rendering
- Tests for edge cases (missing values, conditionals)

## Purpose
Define a comprehensive catalog of error codes with human-readable descriptions and recovery guidance.

## Error Code Structure
```rust
pub struct ErrorCode {
    /// Code like WA-4040
    pub code: String,
    
    /// Error category
    pub category: ErrorCategory,
    
    /// Short title
    pub title: String,
    
    /// Full description
    pub description: String,
    
    /// Why this happens
    pub causes: Vec<String>,
    
    /// How to fix it
    pub recovery_steps: Vec<RecoveryStep>,
    
    /// Related documentation
    pub doc_link: Option<String>,
}

pub enum ErrorCategory {
    WezTerm,      // WA-1xxx
    Storage,      // WA-2xxx
    Pattern,      // WA-3xxx
    Policy,       // WA-4xxx
    Workflow,     // WA-5xxx
    Network,      // WA-6xxx
    Config,       // WA-7xxx
    Internal,     // WA-9xxx
}

pub struct RecoveryStep {
    pub description: String,
    pub command: Option<String>,
}
```

## Error Code Ranges
```
WA-1xxx: WezTerm Errors
  WA-1001: WezTerm CLI not found
  WA-1002: WezTerm not running
  WA-1003: Socket not found
  WA-1010: Pane not found
  WA-1020: Command execution failed
  WA-1030: JSON parse error

WA-2xxx: Storage Errors
  WA-2001: Database initialization failed
  WA-2002: Migration failed
  WA-2010: Sequence discontinuity
  WA-2020: FTS query error

WA-3xxx: Pattern Errors
  WA-3001: Invalid regex
  WA-3002: Pattern pack not found
  WA-3010: Match timeout

WA-4xxx: Policy Errors
  WA-4001: Send blocked - pane in alternate screen
  WA-4002: Send blocked - pane not responding
  WA-4003: Send blocked - rate limit protection
  WA-4010: Approval required
  WA-4020: Action blocked by safety policy

WA-5xxx: Workflow Errors
  WA-5001: Workflow not found
  WA-5002: Workflow aborted
  WA-5010: Guard condition failed
  WA-5020: Pane locked by another workflow

WA-7xxx: Configuration Errors
  WA-7001: Config file not found
  WA-7002: Config parse error
  WA-7010: Validation error
```


## Error Lookup API
```rust
pub struct ErrorCatalog {
    errors: HashMap<String, ErrorCode>,
}

impl ErrorCatalog {
    pub fn get(&self, code: &str) -> Option<&ErrorCode> {
        self.errors.get(code)
    }
    
    pub fn describe(&self, error: &Error) -> ErrorDescription {
        let code = error.code();
        if let Some(catalog_entry) = self.get(&code) {
            ErrorDescription::from_catalog(catalog_entry, error.context())
        } else {
            ErrorDescription::unknown(code, error.message())
        }
    }
}
```

## Implementation

### Event Renderer
```rust
pub struct EventRenderer {
    templates: TemplateRegistry,
}

impl EventRenderer {
    pub fn render(&self, event: &Detection) -> RenderedEvent {
        self.templates.render(event)
    }
    
    pub fn render_for_cli(&self, event: &Detection) -> String {
        let rendered = self.render(event);
        format_for_terminal(&rendered)
    }
    
    pub fn render_compact(&self, event: &Detection) -> String {
        let rendered = self.render(event);
        rendered.summary
    }
}
```

### Error Renderer
```rust
pub struct ErrorRenderer {
    catalog: ErrorCatalog,
}

impl ErrorRenderer {
    pub fn render(&self, error: &Error) -> String {
        let desc = self.catalog.describe(error);
        
        let mut output = format!("Error: {}\n\n", desc.title);
        output.push_str(&desc.description);
        output.push_str("\n\n");
        
        if !desc.suggestions.is_empty() {
            output.push_str("Suggestions:\n");
            for suggestion in &desc.suggestions {
                output.push_str(&format!("  • {}\n", suggestion));
            }
        }
        
        output.push_str(&format!("\nError code: {}\n", desc.code));
        output.push_str(&format!("Run `wa why {}` for more details.\n", desc.code));
        
        output
    }
}
```

```rust
            ErrorDescription::from_catalog(catalog_entry, error.context())
        } else {
            ErrorDescription::unknown(code, error.message())
        }
    }
}
```

### Error Renderer
```rust
pub struct ErrorRenderer {
    catalog: ErrorCatalog,
}

impl ErrorRenderer {
    pub fn render(&self, error: &Error) -> String {
        let desc = self.catalog.describe(error);
        
        let mut output = format!("Error: {}\n\n", desc.title);
        output.push_str(&desc.description);
        output.push_str("\n\n");
        
        if !desc.suggestions.is_empty() {
            output.push_str("Suggestions:\n");
            for suggestion in &desc.suggestions {
                output.push_str(&format!("  • {}\n", suggestion));
            }
        }
        
        output.push_str(&format!("\nError code: {}\n", desc.code));
        output.push_str(&format!("Run `wa why {}` for more details.\n", desc.code));
        
        output
    }
}
```

 {"id":"wa-0go.7","title":"Natural language tests: template rendering, error lookup, CLI output, E2E","description":"# Natural language tests

## Purpose
Comprehensive test coverage for the natural-language event description system:
- template interpolation (variables/conditionals/plurals)
- template registry coverage + fallbacks
- error code catalog completeness
- CLI rendering stability (TTY + non-TTY)

## Test categories

### 1) Template interpolation tests (unit)
Key goals:
- deterministic output (no dependence on wall clock)
- clear handling of missing fields
- correct pluralization/formatting

Example cases:
```rust
#[test]
fn interpolate_simple_variable() {
    let template = "Hello, {name}!";
    let context = hashmap!{"name" => "World"};

    let result = interpolate(template, &context);
    assert_eq!(result, "Hello, World!");
}

#[test]
fn interpolate_missing_variable_preserves_placeholder() {
    let template = "Value: {missing}";
    let context = HashMap::new();

    let result = interpolate(template, &context);
    assert_eq!(result, "Value: {missing}");
}

#[test]
fn interpolate_conditional_true() {
    let template = "{?has_time}Time: {time}{/?has_time}";
    let context = hashmap!{
        "has_time" => Value::Bool(true),
        "time" => Value::String("5 minutes".into()),
    };

    let result = interpolate(template, &context);
    assert_eq!(result, "Time: 5 minutes");
}

#[test]
fn interpolate_plural() {
    let template = "Found {count|item|items}";

    assert_eq!(interpolate(template, &hashmap!{"count" => 1}), "Found 1 item");
    assert_eq!(interpolate(template, &hashmap!{"count" => 5}), "Found 5 items");
}

#[test]
fn format_relative_time_is_deterministic() {
    // Do NOT use Utc::now() in tests.
    // Use a fixed reference time (or a TimeProvider injected into formatter).
    let now = Utc.with_ymd_and_hms(2026, 1, 18, 12, 0, 0).unwrap();
    let value = Value::DateTime(now - chrono::Duration::minutes(5));
    assert_eq!(format_value_with_now(&value, now), "5 minutes ago");
}
```

### 2) Template registry tests (unit)
```rust
#[test]
fn registry_returns_template_for_known_event() {
    let registry = TemplateRegistry::default();
    let template = registry.get("codex.usage_limit_warning");

    assert!(template.summary.contains("{percent}"));
}

#[test]
fn registry_returns_fallback_for_unknown_event() {
    let registry = TemplateRegistry::default();
    let template = registry.get("unknown.event.type");

    assert!(template.summary.contains("Unknown"));
}

#[test]
fn render_event_interpolates_context() {
    let registry = TemplateRegistry::default();
    let event = Detection {
        event_type: "codex.usage_limit_warning".into(),
        extracted: hashmap!{
            "percent" => json!(85),
            "tokens_used" => json!(850000),
        },
        ..Default::default()
    };

    let rendered = registry.render(&event);
    assert!(rendered.summary.contains("85%"));
    assert!(!rendered.summary.contains("{percent}"));
}
```

### 5) E2E integration (standard harness)
This area must NOT rely on `wa watch --simulate` or fixed `sleep N` synchronization.

Requirements:
- Use the standard harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).

## Coverage requirements
- Interpolation: all variable types, conditionals, plurals

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Error catalog includes all shipped WA-XXXX codes.

## Testing

- Snapshot: CLI render output, normalized.
- E2E: adapter case in the shared registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), executed by the standard runner.

 {"id":"wa-1pe","title":"[EPIC] Dry-Run Preview Mode for All Human Commands","description":"# [EPIC] Dry-Run Preview Mode

## Mission
Allow users to **preview any action before executing** it. This builds confidence, enables learning, and prevents mistakes.

## Why This Is Essential
Users are scared to run automation commands because:
- "What if I send to the wrong pane?"
- "What if the workflow does something unexpected?"
- "What if I misconfigured something?"

Dry-run mode eliminates this fear:
- See exactly what would happen
- Verify target resolution is correct
- Understand policy evaluations
- Learn wa's behavior safely

## Scope
Add `--dry-run` flag to all state-modifying human commands:
- `wa send --dry-run`
- `wa workflow run --dry-run`
- `wa approve --dry-run`
- `wa setup --dry-run` (already partially supported)

## How It Works

### wa send --dry-run
```bash
$ wa send --pane 3 --dry-run "Reread AGENTS.md"

DRY RUN - No changes will be made

Target Resolution:
  Pane: 3 (claude_code @ /home/user/project)
  Domain: local

Policy Evaluation:
  ✓ Rate limit: 2/10 sends in last minute (within budget)
  ✓ Pane state: PromptActive (safe to send)
  ✓ No recent gaps (continuity OK)
  ✓ Command safety: text appears safe

Expected Action:
  - Inject 18 characters via wezterm cli send-text --pane-id 3
  - Wait for: prompt boundary (timeout: 30s)

To execute for real:
  wa send --pane 3 "Reread AGENTS.md"
```

### wa workflow run --dry-run
```bash
$ wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 7 --dry-run

DRY RUN - No changes will be made

Workflow: handle_compaction
Target: Pane 7 (codex @ /home/user/project)

Pre-checks:
  ✓ Compaction anchor found in pane tail
  ✓ Pane not in AltScreen
  ✓ No recent output gaps
  ✓ Policy: workflow execution allowed

Steps that would execute:
  1. Acquire workflow lock for pane 7
  2. Stabilize: wait for tail stability (no new deltas for N polls; max 2s)
  3. Send: "Please re-read AGENTS.md...\n"
  4. Verify: wait for prompt boundary
  5. Mark event handled

Estimated completion: Steps 1-5

To execute for real:
  wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 7
```

## Implementation Strategy
1. **DryRun context flag**: Thread `dry_run: bool` through command execution
2. **Policy evaluation without action**: PolicyEngine already separates evaluation from execution
3. **Step plan generation**: Workflow engine generates steps without executing
4. **Consistent output format**: DryRunReport struct for all commands

## Key Design Decisions
- Dry-run output is **structured** (can be JSON for robots)
- Dry-run is **fast** (no waiting for actual execution)
- Dry-run **does not acquire locks** (safe to run in parallel)
- Dry-run **validates inputs** (catches errors early)

## Testing
- Unit tests: dry-run produces expected output for known inputs
- Integration tests: dry-run never modifies state
- Contract tests: dry-run output matches actual execution path

## Success Criteria
- All human commands that modify state support --dry-run
- Dry-run output clearly shows what would happen
- Dry-run never modifies any state (DB, panes, locks)
- Robot mode commands also support dry-run (JSON output)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All state-modifying human commands support --dry-run.
- Dry-run never mutates DB, locks, or panes.

 {"id":"wa-1pe.1","title":"DryRunContext infrastructure: thread dry_run flag through command execution","description":"
# DryRunContext Infrastructure

## Purpose
Create the foundational infrastructure to thread dry_run mode through all command execution paths.

## Implementation

### DryRunContext Struct
```rust
pub struct DryRunContext {
    pub enabled: bool,
    pub report: DryRunReport,
}

pub struct DryRunReport {
    pub command: String,
    pub target_resolution: TargetResolution,
    pub policy_evaluation: PolicyEvaluation,
    pub expected_actions: Vec<PlannedAction>,
    pub warnings: Vec<String>,
}
```

### Threading Pattern
All command handlers receive `CommandContext` which includes `dry_run: bool`.
When dry_run is true:
1. Execute all validation and resolution
2. Build DryRunReport instead of executing
3. Return report as structured output

### CLI Integration
```
$ wa send --dry-run "text"
$ wa workflow run --dry-run handle_compaction
$ wa approve --dry-run <code>
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] DryRunContext and DryRunReport structs defined
- [ ] CommandContext carries dry_run flag from CLI
- [ ] Helper functions for building report sections
- [ ] JSON output for robot mode
- [ ] Human-readable output for TTY

## Testing
- Unit tests for dry_run flag propagation and no-side-effect guards.
- Integration tests: verify no DB/lock/pane mutation under dry-run.
- E2E: add a dry-run scenario with verbose logs and artifacts.

# wa send --dry-run Implementation

## Purpose
Allow users to preview exactly what `wa send` would do before executing.

## Output Format
```
DRY RUN - No changes will be made

Target Resolution:
  Pane: 3 (claude_code @ /home/user/project)
  Domain: local

Policy Evaluation:
  ✓ Rate limit: 2/10 sends in last minute (within budget)
  ✓ Pane state: PromptActive (safe to send)
  ✓ No recent gaps (continuity OK)
  ✓ Command safety: text appears safe

Expected Action:
  - Inject 18 characters via wezterm cli send-text --pane-id 3
  - Wait for: prompt boundary (timeout: 30s)

To execute for real:
  wa send --pane 3 "Reread AGENTS.md"
```

## Implementation Steps
1. Parse and validate all send arguments
2. Resolve target pane (same as real execution)
3. Call PolicyEngine.evaluate() without executing
4. Build PlannedAction with character count and target
5. Format and display report

## Edge Cases
- Multiple targets: show all resolved panes
- Policy denial: show what would block and why
- Ambiguous target: show all candidates and how to narrow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa send --dry-run shows target resolution
- [ ] Policy evaluation displayed with pass/fail
- [ ] Expected WezTerm command shown
- [ ] JSON output for robot mode
- [ ] Helpful "to execute for real" hint

## Testing
- Unit tests for dry_run flag propagation and no-side-effect guards.
- Integration tests: verify no DB/lock/pane mutation under dry-run.
- E2E: add a dry-run scenario with verbose logs and artifacts.

# wa workflow run --dry-run Implementation

## Purpose
Show users exactly what steps a workflow would execute, allowing them to understand and verify workflow behavior before running.

## Output Format
```
DRY RUN - No changes will be made

Workflow: handle_compaction
Target: Pane 7 (codex @ /home/user/project)

Pre-checks:
  ✓ Compaction anchor found in pane tail
  ✓ Pane not in AltScreen
  ✓ No recent output gaps
  ✓ Policy: workflow execution allowed

Steps that would execute:
  1. Acquire workflow lock for pane 7
  2. Stabilize: wait for tail stability (no new deltas for N polls; max 2s)
  3. Send: "Please re-read AGENTS.md..."
  4. Verify: wait for prompt boundary
  5. Mark event handled

Estimated steps: 5

To execute for real:
  wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 7
```

## Implementation Steps
1. Load workflow definition
2. Resolve target pane and verify preconditions
3. Call workflow.plan_steps() to get step list
4. Evaluate each step's policy requirements
5. Build and display step plan report

## Key Considerations
- Don't acquire locks during dry-run
- Show estimated step count
- Highlight steps that might fail policy checks
- Show what event would be marked handled

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa workflow run --dry-run lists all steps
- [ ] Pre-checks shown with pass/fail
- [ ] Each step described with its action
- [ ] Policy-gated steps highlighted
- [ ] JSON output for robot mode

## Testing
- Unit tests for dry_run flag propagation and no-side-effect guards.
- Integration tests: verify no DB/lock/pane mutation under dry-run.
- E2E: add a dry-run scenario with verbose logs and artifacts.

## Purpose

```
DRY RUN - No changes will be made

Approval Token Validation:
  Token: abc123
  Status: ✓ Valid
  Created: 2026-01-18T14:30:00Z
  Expires: 2026-01-18T15:30:00Z

Associated Action:
  Type: SendText
  Pane: 9 (codex @ /project)
  Content: "sudo rm -rf /tmp/cache"
  Reason: RequireApproval (destructive pattern match)

Would grant:
  - Allow-once permission for this specific action
  - Action will execute immediately after approval
  - Audit log entry will be created

To approve for real:
  wa approve abc123
```

## Implementation

2. Look up pending approval request
3. Validate token hasn't expired

5. Don't actually modify approval state

# Dry-Run Testing Suite

## Purpose
Ensure dry-run mode never modifies state and produces accurate predictions.

## Test Categories

### 1. No State Modification Tests
- Dry-run send: DB unchanged, no WezTerm CLI calls
- Dry-run workflow: No locks acquired, no step logs
- Dry-run approve: Approval state unchanged

### 2. Output Correctness Tests
- Target resolution matches actual resolution
- Policy evaluation matches actual evaluation
- Step plan matches actual execution order

### 3. Error Path Tests
- Invalid pane: helpful error in dry-run output
- Policy denial: shows what would block
- Expired approval: shows expiry clearly

### 4. Format Tests
- TTY output is human-readable
- JSON output is valid and complete
- All fields present in both formats

### 5. Contract Tests
- Dry-run output fields match execution fields
- No drift between preview and reality

## Testing Approach
- Mock WezTerm CLI to detect any calls
- Compare dry-run output to actual execution logs
- Snapshot tests for output stability

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 100% coverage of dry-run code paths
- [ ] Verified no state modification in any test
- [ ] Output snapshots for regression detection
- [ ] Error message quality verified

 {"id":"wa-1wg","title":"[EPIC] Backup & Restore: Database Export, Import, Scheduled Backups","description":"# [EPIC] Backup & Restore System

## Mission
Provide users with confidence that their terminal history and configuration will never be lost, and make migration between machines or versions painless.

## Why This Matters
wa accumulates valuable data:
- Terminal transcripts (searchable history)
- Event timeline (debugging context)
- Configuration (tuned patterns, workflows)

Loss of this data would be a significant trust violation. Users need:
- Peace of mind that data is safe
- Easy migration to new machines
- Recovery from corruption or mistakes

## Components

### 1. Database Export (`wa backup export`)
Export the entire database to a portable format:
```bash
$ wa backup export --output backup_2026-01-18.wa
Creating backup...
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Patterns: 23
  Config: included
Backup saved: backup_2026-01-18.wa (45 MB)
SHA256: abc123...
```

Export format:
- Self-describing archive (version header + manifest)
- SQLite dump + JSON metadata
- Optional compression (zstd)
- Integrity checksum

### 2. Database Import (`wa backup import`)
Restore from a backup:
```bash
$ wa backup import backup_2026-01-18.wa --verify
Verifying backup integrity... OK
Backup version: 1.2.0 (compatible)
Contents:
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Config: included

Restore options:
  [1] Full restore (replace current data)
  [2] Merge (keep newer, import older)
  [3] Dry-run (show what would change)
```

Safety:
- Require explicit confirmation for full restore
- Create automatic backup before import
- Validate schema compatibility

### 3. Incremental Backups
For users who want continuous protection:
```toml
[backup]
enabled = true
schedule = "daily"  # or "hourly", "weekly"
retention_days = 30
destination = "~/.local/share/wa/backups/"
max_backups = 10
```

### 4. Configuration Export/Import
Separate from data, for sharing configs:
```bash
$ wa config export --output my-config.toml
$ wa config import my-config.toml --merge
```

### 5. Migration Assistant
When upgrading wa with breaking changes:
```bash
$ wa upgrade --from 1.x
Detected wa 1.x database
Migration required for:
  - Schema v1 → v2 (new event columns)
  - Config format update

Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Success Criteria
A user should be able to:
- create a backup file that is self-contained and verifiable (manifest + checksums)
- restore safely with a preview/dry-run path before any destructive change
- migrate between machines without manual file spelunking

Operational expectations (v0):
- backups are deterministic and explainable (stable manifest, clear versions)
- backups do not leak secrets (config redaction + no raw transcript dumping)
- imports refuse unsafe/incompatible inputs with actionable errors
- scheduled backups are robust (bounded disk usage, retention policy enforced)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Export/import round-trip preserves all data
  - Version detection works across schema versions
  - Merge logic handles conflicts correctly

- Integration tests:
  - Backup with active watcher (snapshot isolation)
  - Import into empty vs populated database
  - Scheduled backup runs correctly

- E2E tests:
  - Full backup/restore cycle with real data
  - Migration from mock old version
  - Corrupt backup rejected with clear error

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa backup export creates portable backup files
- [ ] wa backup import restores with verification
- [ ] Incremental backup scheduling works
- [ ] wa config export/import for config-only migration
- [ ] Version migration assistant for upgrades
- [ ] All backups include integrity checksums
- [ ] Clear error messages for incompatible backups","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:55:22.915697374Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T19:56:24.569572075Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"labels":["area-reliability","area-ux","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1wg","depends_on_id":"wa-37x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:56:40.599665440Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}
